response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, fake content on social media stresses me out more than it probably should. I only spend about an hour a day scrolling through feeds, but honestly that's enough to see how much garbage is out there. Working as a delivery driver in Chicago, I see real life every day, real people, real neighborhoods, and then I go online and see these completely made-up stories getting shared thousands of times. It genuinely bothers me because I know people believe this stuff without questioning it. I'm not great at staying organized or following through on things, so I'll admit I don't always fact-check everything myself, but I've learned to be suspicious of pretty much anything that seems designed to make me angry or scared. That's usually fake or at least twisted around to push some agenda. What really gets under my skin is when I see misinformation about trans issues or healthcare stuff that affects people like me. That's when the anxiety kicks in hard because these lies have real consequences. I've been through a divorce already at twenty-seven, and part of what I learned from that mess is that people will say anything to win an argument or make themselves look better. Social media is the same way. My approach is pretty straightforward even if I don't always stick to it perfectly. I try to avoid sharing things unless I'm absolutely certain they're true, which means I share way less than most people. I also tend to just scroll past the obviously fake stuff rather than engaging with it because arguing online just makes my stress worse. Sometimes I'll check a few different sources if something seems important, but honestly I'd rather spend my limited free time working in my garden or listening to Bach than trying to debunk every piece of nonsense I come across. The platforms themselves need to do better at removing fake content, but I'm not holding my breath waiting for that to happen. For now, I just keep my social media time limited, stay skeptical, and remind myself that most of what I see online isn't worth getting worked up over, even though it still gets to me sometimes.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona has high neuroticism which suggests anxiety and stress about misinformation, low conscientiousness indicating less systematic fact-checking behavior, low openness suggesting preference for familiar approaches and skepticism toward new claims, low extraversion meaning less social engagement online, and low agreeableness suggesting some cynicism. The delivery driver occupation provides real-world grounding. Being transgender and divorced at 27 adds personal stakes to misinformation. Limited social media time and hobbies suggest withdrawal from online stress. Agnostic beliefs may support questioning narratives. The tone should be somewhat anxious, skeptical, straightforward, and personally affected.
response_parsed.tone: anxious, skeptical, straightforward, somewhat cynical, personally affected
